situation: Signal failure at Taman Bahagia station. Monday morning peak 8:30am. All trains halted immediately for safety — signalling system could not confirm train positions, creating collision risk.

outcome.platform_incidents: ~8,500 passengers stranded across 6 stations during 34-minute suspension. Full resumption at reduced 12 trains/hr initially, then restored to 20 trains/hr after 20 minutes.
outcome.verdict: Correct decision to fully stop all trains immediately. Signal failure makes it impossible to know where trains are — running any train risks rear-end collision. Lesson: signal failure = zero trains until system is fully restored and confirmed. No exceptions. Post-restoration, max frequency is mandatory to clear backlog.

situation: Partial signal failure affecting 3 stations on Ampang line. Wednesday afternoon off-peak. Operator attempted to run trains at reduced speed instead of stopping.

outcome.platform_incidents: near-miss incident between two trains at Sri Petaling junction. Emergency stop triggered. Full suspension imposed 12 minutes later.
outcome.verdict: Wrong decision. Continuing service at reduced frequency during signal failure caused a near-miss collision. Any signal failure — partial or full — requires immediate full suspension. The 12-minute delay in halting service was a serious safety breach. Lesson: there is no such thing as a 'safe reduced frequency' during signal failure. Stop all trains immediately and wait for engineering clearance.

situation: Signal system reboot required after software fault detected. Tuesday evening peak 6pm. Engineering team requested 20-minute controlled shutdown to apply patch.

outcome.platform_incidents: orderly evacuation of in-transit passengers at nearest stations. ~5,000 passengers affected. Bus bridge activated within 8 minutes.
outcome.verdict: Textbook response. Full stop immediately upon signal fault detection. Bus bridge activated fast. Service resumed at maximum frequency once system confirmed stable. Total disruption: 23 minutes. Lesson: proactive shutdown is always safer than reactive — catching a signal fault early and stopping immediately prevents worse incidents.

situation: Coldplay Music of the Spheres concert at Bukit Jalil National Stadium. Saturday evening, clear weather. Estimated 80,000 attendance.

outcome.platform_incidents: minor crowding at Bukit Jalil station during 9pm exodus
outcome.verdict: Successful. 14 trains/hr was sufficient. Could have managed with 12 during first half, but surge at concert end justified full deployment throughout.

situation: Hari Raya Aidilfitri eve. Wednesday evening. Clear weather. Mass outstation travel, unusually high ridership across all lines.

outcome.platform_incidents: significant queuing at Masjid Jamek interchange — bottleneck point
outcome.verdict: Insufficient. 12 trains/hr caused overflow at interchange stations. Should have deployed 15 trains/hr from 4pm onwards. Key lesson: holiday eve traffic peaks earlier than expected (4pm not 5pm).

situation: Merdeka Day national celebration at Dataran Merdeka. Saturday. Cloudy. 60,000 expected at parade, heavy pedestrian flow toward city centre.

outcome.verdict: Well managed. 10 trains/hr kept load factor at comfortable 78%. Conservative approach worked because crowd dispersal was gradual (not a single post-event exodus).

situation: New Year's Eve countdown event at KLCC and Dataran Merdeka. Tuesday night, 9pm to 1am. Clear weather.

outcome.platform_incidents: severe overcrowding at KL Sentral and Dang Wangi — passengers waited 3+ trains to board. Complaints filed.
outcome.verdict: Failed. 8 trains/hr severely inadequate for NYE demand. Should have deployed maximum 20 trains/hr. Lesson: late-night events require aggressive frequency even if cost is high, as stranded passengers create safety and reputational risk.

situation: Malaysia Cup football final at Bukit Jalil. Saturday afternoon. Heavy rain. 50,000 attendance.

outcome.platform_incidents: moderate crowding — rain pushed more fans onto LRT instead of driving
outcome.verdict: Adequate but tight. Rain increased LRT ridership beyond prediction — rainy weather consistently adds 15-20% more passengers than forecast. Recommend adding 2 extra trains on top of model prediction whenever weather is rainy for outdoor events.

situation: Normal Friday morning peak hour, no events. Clear weather.

outcome.verdict: Standard operation. 15 trains/hr maintains comfortable 72% load factor during peak. This is the validated baseline for weekday peak hours.

situation: Signal failure on Ampang line caused passenger rerouting to Sri Petaling. Sunday afternoon. Clear weather.

outcome.platform_incidents: queuing at Sri Petaling and Bukit Jalil stations for ~45 minutes until Ampang line restored
outcome.verdict: Marginal. 9 trains/hr nearly reached capacity. Should have immediately deployed 12 trains/hr upon receiving emergency alert. Key lesson: emergency rerouting adds demand faster than standard event surges — act fast.

situation: Chinese New Year eve. Thursday evening. Clear weather. High outstation travel volume, many workers heading home early.

outcome.verdict: Comfortable. 13 trains/hr was slightly more than needed — could have used 11. However, the conservative choice was justified given uncertainty around exact travel timing during festive periods.

situation: Track incident — person on track at Taman Jaya station. Monday morning peak 8am. Service suspended for 22 minutes.

outcome.platform_incidents: severe platform overflow at 4 stations. Estimated 6,000 passengers stranded. Post-clearance recovery required max frequency for 2 hours.
outcome.verdict: Service correctly suspended during incident. However post-resumption frequency was only raised to 14 trains/hr — insufficient to clear backlog. Should have immediately deployed max 20 trains/hr for at least 90 minutes post-clearance. Lesson: backlog grows by ~10,000 passengers per 10 minutes of suspension during peak hours.

situation: Power failure at substation affecting Kelana Jaya to Putra Heights section. Tuesday evening peak. Rainy weather.

outcome.platform_incidents: large crowds stranded at Kelana Jaya and Subang Jaya. Bus replacement service overwhelmed.
outcome.verdict: Power failure during rain severely impacted operations. 3 trains/hr on backup power was insufficient. Partial restoration at 45 minutes allowed 8 trains/hr. Lesson: always activate bus bridge immediately upon power failure — LRT alone cannot handle even reduced demand under backup power.

situation: New Year's Day morning. Wednesday. Residual crowd from overnight NYE events. Stormy weather from 6am.

outcome.platform_incidents: none — storm kept most people indoors
outcome.verdict: Conservative was correct. Storm significantly reduced ridership despite NYE residual. 7 trains/hr adequate. Stormy weather suppresses demand more than expected — frequency cap for safety also coincidentally matched actual demand.

situation: Train breakdown mid-service between Chan Sow Lin and Miharja during Thursday evening peak. One train taken out of service.

outcome.platform_incidents: minor delays of 8-12 minutes at 3 stations. Recovered within 35 minutes.
outcome.verdict: Well handled. Reducing from 13 to 11 trains/hr after breakdown maintained flow without overcrowding. Passengers redistributed naturally across remaining trains. Lesson: one train loss during peak is manageable if remaining fleet is redeployed with tighter spacing.

situation: Taylor Swift Eras Tour concert at Bukit Jalil. Saturday night, 7pm to 11pm. Clear weather. Estimated 65,000 attendance.

outcome.platform_incidents: managed queuing at Bukit Jalil during 10:30pm exodus — cleared within 25 minutes
outcome.verdict: Successful. 17 trains/hr handled the surge well. Pre-concert arrival (8pm) was lighter than expected — most fans arrived by car. Post-concert exodus was the critical window. Key lesson: for mega-concerts at Bukit Jalil, prioritise post-event frequency over pre-event.

situation: KLCC fireworks display, Saturday night 9pm. Rainy weather from 7pm. Estimated 30,000 spectators.

outcome.platform_incidents: heavy crowding at KLCC station. Rain drove most spectators to LRT instead of taxis.
outcome.verdict: Nearly overwhelmed. Rain multiplied ridership beyond model prediction — demand was 30% higher than dry-weather estimate. Should have deployed 16 trains/hr when rain forecast was confirmed. Lesson: always add buffer trains when outdoor event coincides with rainy weather.

situation: Valentine's Day evening. Wednesday. Clear weather. No formal events but unusually high leisure ridership to malls and restaurants.

outcome.verdict: Appropriate. Moderate option correctly handled the mild uplift in ridership. Valentine's Day creates diffuse demand spread across many destinations — unlike concerts, no single station bottleneck. Standard plus 1-2 extra trains is sufficient.
